name: Lake Gleason (South Side)
short_description: Scenic lakeside rockhounding spot in Pike County offering quartz, jasper, and chert specimens along gentle shoreline terrain. Public access with minimal restrictions makes this ideal for casual col...
description:
Lake Gleason's south side provides a relaxed rockhounding experience in Pike County's Ouachita Mountains region. The lake's shoreline exposes sedimentary formations and weathered quartz deposits that have accumulated along the water's edge, creating excellent opportunities for surface collection. The terrain features gentle slopes descending to the lake, with exposed banks and rocky outcrops yielding specimens throughout the year.

The geology here reflects the Paleozoic era's marine environments, where silica-rich sediments and volcanic activity deposited the quartz and jasper materials visible today. Chert nodules and fragments scatter along the shoreline, formed through diagenesis of silica-rich oozes in ancient shallow seas. The mild climate and moderate elevation (618 feet) mean good collecting conditions persist across multiple seasons.

Collectors should focus on areas where wave action and seasonal water level changes have exposed fresh material along the bank. The south side orientation provides natural erosion patterns that continuously reveal new specimens. Expect to find primarily small to medium-sized quartz pieces, banded jasper fragments, and translucent chert suitable for display or lapidary work.

This location works well for beginners and families due to gentle terrain and straightforward specimen identification. The combination of lake views, accessible parking, and productive collecting makes Lake Gleason a reliable choice for Pike County rockhounding trips.
history: Lake Gleason sits within the Ouachita Mountains, part of the broader Ouachita orogeny that formed during the Paleozoic era's Alleghenian mountain-building event. The quartz and jasper found here originated from silica-rich marine sediments and volcanic ash deposits that metamorphosed under pressure, later exposed by erosion and now concentrated along the modern lakeshore through water action and weathering.
tips: Search the rocky shoreline immediately below the waterline and along any exposed banks, especially where wave action has recently cleared vegetation and loose soil. Bring a small classifier or mesh bag to rinse specimens in the lake water and assess clarity and color before collecting. Look for translucent quartz pieces with natural facets, banded jasper showing red or brown striping, and solid gray to tan chert nodules that feel glassy and sharp to the touch.
terrain_notes: Gentle slope terrain at 618 feet elevation. Shoreline access with rocky footing on weathered stone and sediment. Walking distance from parking estimated at 50-200 feet depending on chosen collection area. Moderate footing difficulty due to uneven rocks and potential muddy banks near water. Some areas may be soft or slippery during wet seasons.
best_season: March - May, September - November. Arkansas's mild springs and falls provide comfortable collecting temperatures and lower lake levels that expose more shoreline area. Summer heat and humidity can be intense; winter is passable but water access may be limited.
written_directions: From Murfreesboro, take AR 27 south toward Pike County. Continue approximately 12-15 miles south. Look for Lake Gleason access signs or local landmarks. The south side access is typically reached via county roads; consult local signage as road names vary. Parking areas near the south shore provide direct lake access. GPS coordinates 34.153583, -93.709935 mark the general collecting area.
road_conditions: Paved roads to main area; final access may include unpaved county roads in good condition. Passenger vehicles suitable.
parking_notes: Informal parking pullouts near lake access points. No developed parking lot observed. Space for 2-4 vehicles at most pull-offs. Arrive early during peak seasons.
collection_rules: Personal collection only. Respect private property boundaries around the lake. Do not disturb bank erosion controls or vegetation stabilization efforts. Collect only loose surface specimens; do not extract from living rock or use tools to break formations. Pack out all trash. Follow posted signage regarding access restrictions.
rules:
Personal collection only
No commercial collection
Respect property boundaries
Do not break living rock or use extraction tools
Pack out all trash
Follow all posted access signs
nearest_services: Murfreesboro, approximately 12-15 miles north. Gas stations, restaurants, and supplies available there.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently sloping terrain. Rocky shoreline footing requires sturdy shoes but no technical climbing. Not wheelchair accessible due to uneven terrain and lack of paved paths. Accessible to most mobile hikers and families with moderate fitness.

faq.question: What gemstones and minerals can you find at Lake Gleason South Side in Arkansas?
faq.answer: Lake Gleason's south shore yields quartz, jasper, and chert specimens. Quartz appears as translucent fragments and points; jasper displays banded patterns in reds, browns, and tans; chert forms gray to tan nodules with a glassy fracture. Wave action continuously exposes fresh material along the shoreline.

faq.question: Is Lake Gleason South Side free to rockhound, and do you need a permit?
faq.answer: Yes, Lake Gleason South Side is free public rockhounding with no permit required. Personal collection is allowed on public shoreline. Respect private property boundaries and follow any posted access restrictions around the lake.

faq.question: When is the best time to go rockhounding at Lake Gleason in Pike County?
faq.answer: March through May and September through November offer ideal conditions. Spring and fall provide comfortable temperatures and lower lake levels that expose more shoreline for collecting. Summer heat can be intense; winter is passable but water access may be limited.

faq.question: How do you get to Lake Gleason South Side from Murfreesboro, Arkansas?
faq.answer: From Murfreesboro, take AR 27 south approximately 12-15 miles toward Pike County. Follow local signage and county roads to the south shore access. Informal parking pullouts near the lake provide direct shoreline access. GPS coordinates 34.153583, -93.709935 pinpoint the collecting area.

faq.question: Is Lake Gleason rockhounding site good for beginners and families with kids?
faq.answer: Lake Gleason South Side is excellent for beginners and families. The easy terrain, gentle slopes, and accessible shoreline require no technical skills. Children ages 6 and up can participate safely with supervision. Specimens are easy to identify, and the scenic lake setting makes for a pleasant outing.

faq.question: What tools and tips do you need for collecting quartz and jasper at Lake Gleason?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking boots for rocky footing, a mesh bag or classifier to rinse specimens in the lake, and a small hand tool for loosening sediment (no extraction from solid rock). Search along wave-exposed banks and the waterline where freshly cleared material appears. Look for translucent quartz with natural facets and banded jasper with distinct color striping.
